(Kannada: [[ˈkənːəɖa]](/wiki/Help:IPA "Help:IPA") [ⓘ](/wiki/File:Kannada-pronounciation.ogg "File:Kannada-pronounciation.ogg"))[[4]](#cite_note-WesleyanMissionPress1858-4) is a [Dravidian language](/wiki/Dravidian_languages "Dravidian languages") spoken predominantly in the state of [Karnataka](/wiki/Karnataka "Karnataka") in southwestern [India](/wiki/India "India"), and spoken by a minority of the population in all neighbouring states. It has 44 million native speakers, and is additionally a second or third language for 15 million speakers in Karnataka.[[1]](#cite_note-e27-1) It is the official and administrative language of Karnataka.[[5]](#cite_note-official-5) It also has [scheduled status](/wiki/Languages_with_legal_status_in_India "Languages with legal status in India") in India and has been included among the country's [designated classical languages](/wiki/Classical_languages_of_India "Classical languages of India").[[6]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEKuiper201174-6)[[7]](#cite_note-oldest-7)

**Kannada** (Kannada: [[ˈkənːəɖa]](/wiki/Help:IPA "Help:IPA") [ⓘ](/wiki/File:Kannada-pronounciation.ogg "File:Kannada-pronounciation.ogg"))[[4]](#cite_note-WesleyanMissionPress1858-4) is a [Dravidian language](/wiki/Dravidian_languages "Dravidian languages") spoken predominantly in the state of [Karnataka](/wiki/Karnataka "Karnataka") in southwestern [India](/wiki/India "India"), and spoken by a minority of the population in all neighbouring states. It has 44 million native speakers, and is additionally a second or third language for 15 million speakers in Karnataka.[[1]](#cite_note-e27-1) It is the official and administrative language of Karnataka.[[5]](#cite_note-official-5) It also has [scheduled status](/wiki/Languages_with_legal_status_in_India "Languages with legal status in India") in India and has been included among the country's [designated classical languages](/wiki/Classical_languages_of_India "Classical languages of India").[[6]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEKuiper201174-6)[[7]](#cite_note-oldest-7)

Kannada was the court language of a number of dynasties and empires of [South India](/wiki/South_India "South India"), [Central India](/wiki/Central_India "Central India") and the [Deccan Plateau](/wiki/Deccan_Plateau "Deccan Plateau"), namely the [Kadamba dynasty](/wiki/Kadamba_dynasty "Kadamba dynasty"), [Western Ganga dynasty](/wiki/Western_Ganga_dynasty "Western Ganga dynasty"), [Nolamba dynasty](/wiki/Nolamba_dynasty "Nolamba dynasty"), [Chalukya dynasty](/wiki/Chalukya_dynasty "Chalukya dynasty"), [Rashtrakutas](/wiki/Rashtrakutas "Rashtrakutas"),[[8]](#cite_note-:0-8) [Western Chalukya Empire](/wiki/Western_Chalukya_Empire "Western Chalukya Empire"), [Seuna dynasty](/wiki/Seuna_(Yadava)_dynasty "Seuna (Yadava) dynasty"), [Kingdom of Mysore](/wiki/Kingdom_of_Mysore "Kingdom of Mysore"),[[9]](#cite_note-9) [Nayakas of Keladi](/wiki/Nayakas_of_Keladi "Nayakas of Keladi"),[[10]](#cite_note-10) [Hoysala dynasty](/wiki/Hoysala_kingdom "Hoysala kingdom") and the [Vijayanagara Empire](/wiki/Vijayanagara_Empire "Vijayanagara Empire").

The Kannada language is written using the [Kannada script](/wiki/Kannada_script "Kannada script"), which evolved from the 5th-century [Kadamba script](/wiki/Kadamba_script "Kadamba script"). Kannada is attested [epigraphically](/wiki/Epigraphy "Epigraphy") for about one and a half millennia and literary [Old Kannada](/wiki/Rashtrakuta_literature "Rashtrakuta literature") flourished during the 9th-century [Rashtrakuta Empire](/wiki/Rashtrakuta_Empire "Rashtrakuta Empire").[[11]](#cite_note-11)[[12]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEZvelebil19737-12) Kannada has an [unbroken literary history](/wiki/Kannada_literature "Kannada literature") of around 1200 years.[[13]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEGarg199267-13) Kannada literature has been presented with eight [Jnanpith Awards](/wiki/Jnanpith_Award "Jnanpith Award"), the most for any Dravidian language and the second highest for any Indian language,[[14]](#cite_note-14)[[15]](#cite_note-15)[[16]](#cite_note-16) and one [International Booker Prize](/wiki/International_Booker_Prize "International Booker Prize").[[17]](#cite_note-17) In July 2011, a center for the study of classical Kannada was established as part of the [Central Institute of Indian Languages](/wiki/Central_Institute_of_Indian_Languages "Central Institute of Indian Languages") in [Mysore](/wiki/Mysore "Mysore") to facilitate research related to the language.[[18]](#cite_note-18)

## Geographic distribution

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Kannada&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Geographic distribution")]

Kannada had 43.7[[8]](#cite_note-:0-8) million native speakers in [India](/wiki/India "India") at the time of the 2011 census. It is the main language of the state of [Karnataka](/wiki/Karnataka "Karnataka"), where it is spoken natively by 40.6 million people, or about two-thirds of the state's population.

There are native Kannada speakers in the neighbouring states of [Tamil Nadu](/wiki/Tamil_Nadu "Tamil Nadu") (1,140,000 speakers), [Maharashtra](/wiki/Maharashtra "Maharashtra") (993,000), [Andhra Pradesh](/wiki/Andhra_Pradesh "Andhra Pradesh") and [Telangana](/wiki/Telangana "Telangana") (533,000), [Kerala](/wiki/Kerala "Kerala") (78,100), and [Goa](/wiki/Goa "Goa") (67,800).[[19]](#cite_note-19)

It is also spoken as a second and third language by over 12.9 million non-native speakers in Karnataka.[[20]](#cite_note-2001census-20)

Kannadigas form Tamil Nadu's third-largest linguistic group; their population is roughly 1.23 million, which is 2.2% of Tamil Nadu's total population.[[21]](#cite_note-TimesofIndia-21)[[22]](#cite_note-22)

The [Malayalam](/wiki/Malayalam "Malayalam") spoken by people of [Lakshadweep](/wiki/Lakshadweep "Lakshadweep") has many Kannada words.[[23]](#cite_note-23)

In the United States, there were 35,900 speakers in 2006–2008,[[24]](#cite_note-24) a number that had risen to 48,600 by 2015.

There are 4,000 speakers in Canada (2016 census), 9,700 in Australia (2016 census), 22,000 in Singapore (2018 estimate),[[25]](#cite_note-Ethnologue_kan-25) and 59,000 in Malaysia (2021 estimate).[[25]](#cite_note-Ethnologue_kan-25)[*[better source needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Verifiability#Questionable_sources "Wikipedia:Verifiability")*]

## Development

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Kannada&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Development")]

Kannada, like [Malayalam](/wiki/Malayalam "Malayalam") and [Tamil](/wiki/Tamil_language "Tamil language"), is a [South Dravidian language](/wiki/South_Dravidian_language "South Dravidian language") and a descendant of [Tamil-Kannada](/wiki/Tamil-Kannada "Tamil-Kannada"), from which it derives its grammar and core vocabulary. Its history can be divided into three stages: Old Kannada, or *Haḷegannaḍa* from 450 to 1200 AD, Middle Kannada (*Naḍugannaḍa*) from 1200 to 1700 and Modern Kannada (*Hosagannaḍa*) from 1700 to the present.[[26]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESteever1998129-26)[[27]](#cite_note-R._Narasimhacharya_1934_1-27)

Kannada has been considerably influenced by [Sanskrit](/wiki/Sanskrit "Sanskrit") and [Prakrit](/wiki/Prakrit "Prakrit")—in morphology, phonetics, vocabulary, grammar, and syntax. The three principal sources of influence on literary Kannada grammar appear to be [Pāṇini](/wiki/P%C4%81%E1%B9%87ini "Pāṇini")'s grammar, non-Pāṇinian schools of Sanskrit grammar, particularly *Katantra* and *Sakatayana* schools, and [Prakrit](/wiki/Prakrit "Prakrit") grammar.[[28]](#cite_note-prak-28) Literary Prakrit seems to have prevailed in [Karnataka](/wiki/Karnataka "Karnataka") since ancient times. Speakers of vernacular Prakrit may have come into contact with Kannada speakers, thus influencing their language, even before Kannada was used for administrative or liturgical purposes.[[28]](#cite_note-prak-28)[[29]](#cite_note-Studies-29)[[27]](#cite_note-R._Narasimhacharya_1934_1-27) The scholar K. V. Narayana claims that many tribal languages now designated as Kannada dialects could be nearer to the earlier form of the language, with lesser influence from other languages.[[30]](#cite_note-civil-30)

The work of the scholar [Iravatham Mahadevan](/wiki/Iravatham_Mahadevan "Iravatham Mahadevan") indicates that Kannada was already a language of rich spoken tradition by the 3rd century BC. Based on native Kannada words in Prakrit inscriptions of that period, Kannada must have been spoken by a broad and stable population.[[30]](#cite_note-civil-30)[[31]](#cite_note-Tamil_epigraphy1-31)[[32]](#cite_note-32)

Kannada includes many loan words from Sanskrit. Some unaltered loan words ([Sanskrit](/wiki/Sanskrit_language "Sanskrit language"): तत्सम, [romanized](/wiki/Romanization_of_Sanskrit "Romanization of Sanskrit"): *[tatsama](/wiki/Tatsama "Tatsama")*, [lit.](/wiki/Literal_translation "Literal translation") 'same as that'') include *dina*, 'day', *kōpa*, 'anger', *sūrya*, 'sun', *mukha*, 'face', and *nimiṣa*, 'minute'.[[33]](#cite_note-tatsama-33) Some examples of naturalised Sanskrit words ([Sanskrit](/wiki/Sanskrit_language "Sanskrit language"): तद्भव, [romanized](/wiki/Romanization_of_Sanskrit "Romanization of Sanskrit"): *tadbhava*, [lit.](/wiki/Literal_translation "Literal translation") 'arising from that') in Kannada are *varṇa*, 'colour', *pūrṇime*, and *rāya* from *rāja*, 'king'.[[34]](#cite_note-banna-34) Some naturalised words of Prakrit origin in Kannada are *baṇṇa*, 'colour' derived from *vaṇṇa*, *huṇṇime*, 'full moon' from *puṇṇivā*.

## History

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Kannada&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: History")]

### Early traces

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Kannada&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Early traces")]

Main articles: [Halmidi inscription](/wiki/Halmidi_inscription "Halmidi inscription"), [Kappe Arabhatta](/wiki/Kappe_Arabhatta "Kappe Arabhatta"), [Shravanabelagola inscription of Nandisena](/wiki/Shravanabelagola_inscription_of_Nandisena "Shravanabelagola inscription of Nandisena"), [Tyagada Brahmadeva Pillar](/wiki/Tyagada_Brahmadeva_Pillar "Tyagada Brahmadeva Pillar"), [Atakur inscription](/wiki/Atakur_inscription "Atakur inscription"), [Doddahundi nishidhi inscription](/wiki/Doddahundi_nishidhi_inscription "Doddahundi nishidhi inscription"), and [List of people associated with the study of Kannada inscriptions](/wiki/List_of_people_associated_with_the_study_of_Kannada_inscriptions "List of people associated with the study of Kannada inscriptions")

The [Halmidi inscription](/wiki/Halmidi_inscription "Halmidi inscription") at [Halmidi](/wiki/Halmidi "Halmidi") village, in old-Kannada, is usually dated to 450 AD ([Kadamba Dynasty](/wiki/Kadamba_Dynasty "Kadamba Dynasty")).

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/67/6th_century_Kannada_inscription_in_cave_temple_number_3_at_Badami.jpg/250px-6th_century_Kannada_inscription_in_cave_temple_number_3_at_Badami.jpg)](/wiki/File:6th_century_Kannada_inscription_in_cave_temple_number_3_at_Badami.jpg)

Old-Kannada inscription dated 578 AD (Badami Chalukya dynasty), outside Badami cave no.3

The earliest Kannada inscriptions are from the middle of the 5th century AD, but there are a number of earlier texts that may have been influenced by the ancestor language of Old Kannada.[[35]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBhat1993102,103-35)

Iravatam Mahadevan, author of a work on early Tamil epigraphy, argued that oral traditions in Kannada and Telugu existed much before written documents were produced. Although the rock inscriptions of Ashoka were written in Prakrit, the spoken language in those regions was Kannada as the case may be. He can be quoted as follows:[[36]](#cite_note-civil2-36)

> If proof were needed to show that Kannada was the spoken language of the region during the early period, one needs only to study the large number of Kannada personal names and place names in the early Prakrit inscriptions on stone and copper in Upper South India [...] Kannada was spoken by relatively large and well-settled populations, living in well-organised states ruled by able dynasties like the Satavahanas, with a high degree of civilisation [...] There is, therefore, no reason to believe that these languages had less rich or less expressive oral traditions than Tamil had towards the end of its pre-literate period.

Kannada linguist, historian and researcher B. A. Viveka Rai and Kannada writer, lyricist, and linguist Doddarangegowda assert that due to the extensive trade relations that existed between the ancient Kannada lands ([Kuntalas](/wiki/Kuntala_country "Kuntala country"), [Mahishakas](/wiki/Mahisha_kingdom "Mahisha kingdom"), Punnatas, [Mahabanas](/wiki/Bana_kingdom "Bana kingdom"), [Asmakas](/wiki/Asmaka "Asmaka"), etc.) and Greece, Egypt, the [Hellenistic](/wiki/Hellenistic_period "Hellenistic period") and [Roman](/wiki/Roman_Republic "Roman Republic") empires and others, there was exchange of people, ideas, literature, etc. and a Kannada book existed in the form of a palm-leaf manuscript in the old Alexandria library which was subsequently lost in the fire. They state that this also proves that the Kannada language and literature must have flourished much before the library was established in between c. 285-48 BC. This document played a vital role in getting the classical status to Kannada from the Indian Central Government.[[37]](#cite_note-37) The [Ashoka rock edict](/wiki/Ashoka_rock_edict "Ashoka rock edict") found at [Brahmagiri](/wiki/Brahmagiri_archaeological_site "Brahmagiri archaeological site") (dated to 250 BC) has been suggested to contain words (*Isila*, meaning to throw, viz. an arrow, etc.) in identifiable Kannada.[[38]](#cite_note-isila-38)[[39]](#cite_note-39)[[40]](#cite_note-deccanherald-40)

In some 3rd–1st century BC Tamil inscriptions, words of Kannada influence such as *Naliyura*, *kavuDi* and *posil* were found. In a 3rd-century AD Tamil inscription there is usage of *oppanappa vIran*. Here the honorific *appa* to a person's name is an influence from Kannada. Another word of Kannada origin is *taayviru* and is found in a 4th-century AD Tamil inscription. S. Settar studied the *sittanavAsal* inscription of first century AD as also the inscriptions at *tirupparamkunram*, *adakala* and *neDanUpatti*. The later inscriptions were studied in detail by Iravatham Mahadevan also. Mahadevan argues that the words *erumi*, *kavuDi*, *poshil* and *tAyiyar* have their origin in Kannada because Tamil cognates are not available. Settar adds the words *nADu* and *iLayar* to this list. Mahadevan feels that some grammatical categories found in these inscriptions are also unique to Kannada rather than Tamil. Both these scholars attribute these influences to the movements and spread of Jainas in these regions. These inscriptions belong to the period between the first century BC and fourth century AD. These are some examples that are proof of the early usage of a few Kannada origin words in early [Tamil](/wiki/Tamil_language "Tamil language") inscriptions before the common era and in the early centuries of the common era.[[31]](#cite_note-Tamil_epigraphy1-31)

[Pliny the Elder](/wiki/Pliny_the_Elder "Pliny the Elder"), a Roman historian, wrote about pirates between [Muziris](/wiki/Muziris "Muziris") and Nitrias ([Netravati River](/wiki/Netravati_River "Netravati River")), called Nitran by Ptolemy. He also mentions Barace (Barcelore), referring to the modern port city of [Mangaluru](/wiki/Mangaluru "Mangaluru"), upon its mouth. Many of these are Kannada origin names of places and rivers of the Karnataka coast of the 1st century AD.[[41]](#cite_note-41)[[42]](#cite_note-42)[[43]](#cite_note-43)

The Greek geographer [Ptolemy](/wiki/Ptolemy "Ptolemy") (150 AD) mentions places such as Badiamaioi (Badami), Inde (Indi), Kalligeris (Kalkeri), Modogoulla (Mudagal), Petrigala (Pattadakal), Hippokoura (Huvina Hipparagi), Nagarouris (Nagur), Tabaso (Tavasi), Tiripangalida (Gadahinglai), Soubouttou or Sabatha (Savadi), Banaouase (Banavasi), Thogorum (Tagara), Biathana (Paithan), Sirimalaga (Malkhed), Aloe (Ellapur) and Pasage (Palasige).[[44]](#cite_note-44) He mentions a Satavahana king Sire Polemaios, who is identified with Sri Pulumayi (or Pulumavi), whose name is derived from the Kannada word for *Puli*, meaning tiger. Some scholars indicate that the name Pulumayi is actually Kannada's *Puli Maiyi* or *One with the body of a tiger* indicating native Kannada origin for the Satavahanas.[[45]](#cite_note-45) Pai identifies all the 10 cities mentioned by Ptolemy (100–170 AD) as lying between the river Benda (or Binda) or [Bhima river](/wiki/Bhima_River "Bhima River") in the north and Banaouasei ([Banavasi](/wiki/Banavasi "Banavasi")) in the south, viz. Nagarouris (Nagur), Tabaso (Tavasi), Inde ([Indi](/wiki/Indi,_Karnataka "Indi, Karnataka")), Tiripangalida ([Gadhinglaj](/wiki/Gadhinglaj "Gadhinglaj")), Hippokoura ([Huvina Hipparagi](/wiki/Huvina_Hipparagi "Huvina Hipparagi")), Soubouttou ([Savadi](/wiki/Savadi,_Gadag "Savadi, Gadag")), Sirimalaga ([Malkhed](/wiki/Malkheda "Malkheda")), Kalligeris ([Kalkeri](/wiki/Kalkeri "Kalkeri")), Modogoulla ([Mudgal](/wiki/Mudgal "Mudgal")), and Petirgala ([Pattadakal](/wiki/Pattadakal "Pattadakal"))—as being located in Northern Karnataka, which explain the existence of Kannada place names (and the language and culture) in the southern Kuntala region during the reign of [Vasishtiputra Pulumayi](/wiki/Vasishthiputra_Pulumavi "Vasishthiputra Pulumavi") (c. 85–125 AD, i.e., late 1st century – early 2nd century AD) who was ruling from Paithan in the north and his son, prince Vilivaya-kura or Pulumayi Kumara was ruling from Huvina Hipparagi in present Karnataka in the south.[[46]](#cite_note-46)

An early ancestor of Kannada (or a related language) may have been spoken by Indian traders in [Roman-era Egypt](/wiki/Roman_Egypt "Roman Egypt") and it may account for the Indian-language passages in the ancient Greek play known as the [Charition mime](/wiki/Charition_mime "Charition mime").[[47]](#cite_note-Salomon1991-47)

### Epigraphy

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Kannada&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: Epigraphy")]

The earliest examples of a full-length Kannada language stone inscription (*śilāśāsana*) containing [Brahmi](/wiki/Brahmi "Brahmi") characters with characteristics attributed to those of [proto-Kannada](/wiki/Halegannada "Halegannada") in *Haḷe Kannaḍa* (*lit* Old Kannada) script can be found in the [Halmidi inscription](/wiki/Halmidi_inscription "Halmidi inscription"), usually dated c. 450 AD, indicating that Kannada had become an administrative language at that time. The Halmidi inscription provides invaluable information about the history and culture of Karnataka.[[48]](#cite_note-admin-48)[[49]](#cite_note-hal-49)[[50]](#cite_note-Oldest_inscription-50)[[51]](#cite_note-ind-51) A set of five copper plate inscriptions discovered in Mudiyanur, though in the [Sanskrit](/wiki/Sanskrit "Sanskrit") language, is in the [Pre-Old Kannada script](/wiki/Kadamba_script "Kadamba script") older than the Halmidi edict date of 450 AD, as per palaeographers.

Followed by [B. L. Rice](/wiki/B._Lewis_Rice "B. Lewis Rice"), leading epigrapher and historian, K. R. Narasimhan following a detailed study and comparison, declared that the plates belong to the 4th century, i.e., 338 AD.[[52]](#cite_note-52)[[53]](#cite_note-53)[[54]](#cite_note-54)[[55]](#cite_note-55)[[56]](#cite_note-56)[[57]](#cite_note-57) The Kannada Lion balustrade inscription excavated at the Pranaveshwara temple complex at Talagunda near Shiralakoppa of Shivamogga district, dated to 370 AD is now considered the earliest Kannada inscriptions replacing the Halmidi inscription of 450 AD.[[58]](#cite_note-58) The 5th century poetic Tamatekallu inscription of [Chitradurga](/wiki/Chitradurga "Chitradurga") and the Siragunda inscription from [Chikkamagaluru](/wiki/Chikkamagaluru "Chikkamagaluru") Taluk of 500 AD are further examples.[[59]](#cite_note-chikka-59)[[60]](#cite_note-rice-60)[[61]](#cite_note-tamate-61) Recent reports indicate that the [Old Kannada](/wiki/Halegannada "Halegannada") *Gunabhushitana* *Nishadi* inscription discovered on the Chandragiri hill, [Shravanabelagola](/wiki/Shravanabelagola "Shravanabelagola"), is older than Halmidi inscription by about fifty to hundred years and may belong to the period AD 350–400.[[62]](#cite_note-62)

The noted archaeologist and art historian S. Shettar is of the opinion that an inscription of the [Western Ganga](/wiki/Western_Ganga_Dynasty "Western Ganga Dynasty") King Kongunivarma Madhava (c. 350–370) found at Tagarthi (Tyagarthi) in Shikaripura taluk of Shimoga district is of 350 AD and is also older than the Halmidi inscription.[[63]](#cite_note-konga-63)[[64]](#cite_note-64)

Current estimates of the total number of existing [epigraphs](/wiki/Epigraphy "Epigraphy") written in Kannada range from 30,000 by the scholar [Sheldon Pollock](/wiki/Sheldon_Pollock "Sheldon Pollock") to over 35,000 by Amaresh Datta of the [Sahitya Akademi](/wiki/Sahitya_Akademi "Sahitya Akademi").[[65]](#cite_note-current-65)[[66]](#cite_note-dense-66) Prior to the Halmidi inscription, there is an abundance of inscriptions containing Kannada words, phrases and sentences, proving its antiquity. The 543 AD Badami cliff inscription of [Pulakesi I](/wiki/Pulakesi_I "Pulakesi I") is an example of a Sanskrit inscription in old Kannada script.[[67]](#cite_note-cliff-67)[[68]](#cite_note-cliff1-68)

Kannada inscriptions are discovered in [Andhra Pradesh](/wiki/Andhra_Pradesh "Andhra Pradesh") and [Telangana](/wiki/Telangana "Telangana"), [Maharashtra](/wiki/Maharashtra "Maharashtra"), [Tamil Nadu](/wiki/Tamil_Nadu "Tamil Nadu"), [Madhya Pradesh](/wiki/Madhya_Pradesh "Madhya Pradesh") and [Gujarat](/wiki/Gujarat "Gujarat") in addition to [Karnataka](/wiki/Karnataka "Karnataka"). This indicates the spread of the influence of the language over the ages, especially during the rule of large Kannada empires.[[69]](#cite_note-jura-69)[[70]](#cite_note-70)[[71]](#cite_note-71)[[72]](#cite_note-72)

The earliest copper plates inscribed in Old Kannada script and language, dated to the early 8th century AD, are associated with [Alupa](/wiki/Alupas "Alupas") King Aluvarasa II from Belmannu (the Dakshina Kannada district), and display the double crested fish, his royal emblem.[[73]](#cite_note-Kannada_copperplate-73) The oldest well-preserved palm leaf manuscript in *Old Kannada* is that of *Dhavala*. It dates to around the 9th century and is preserved in the Jain Bhandar, Mudbidri, [Dakshina Kannada](/wiki/Dakshina_Kannada "Dakshina Kannada") district.[[74]](#cite_note-Palm_leaf_manuscript-74) The manuscript contains 1478 leaves written using ink.[[74]](#cite_note-Palm_leaf_manuscript-74)

### Coins

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Kannada&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: Coins")]

Some early [Kadamba Dynasty](/wiki/Kadamba_Dynasty "Kadamba Dynasty") coins bearing the Kannada inscription *Vira* and *Skandha* were found in Satara collectorate.[[75]](#cite_note-sat-75) A gold coin bearing three inscriptions of *Sri* and an abbreviated inscription of king Bhagiratha's name called *bhagi* (c. 390–420 AD) in old Kannada exists.[[76]](#cite_note-bhagi-76) A Kadamba copper coin dated to the 5th century AD with the inscription *Srimanaragi* in Kannada script was discovered in Banavasi, [Uttara Kannada district](/wiki/Uttara_Kannada_district "Uttara Kannada district").[[77]](#cite_note-Kadamba_coin-77) Coins with Kannada legends have been discovered spanning the rule of the [Western Ganga Dynasty](/wiki/Western_Ganga_Dynasty "Western Ganga Dynasty"), the Badami [Chalukyas](/wiki/Chalukyas "Chalukyas"), the [Alupas](/wiki/Alupas "Alupas"), the [Western Chalukyas](/wiki/Western_Chalukyas "Western Chalukyas"), the [Rashtrakutas](/wiki/Rashtrakutas "Rashtrakutas"), the [Hoysalas](/wiki/Hoysalas "Hoysalas"), the [Vijayanagar Empire](/wiki/Vijayanagar_Empire "Vijayanagar Empire"), the [Kadamba Dynasty](/wiki/Kadamba_Dynasty "Kadamba Dynasty") of Banavasi, the [Keladi Nayakas](/wiki/Keladi_Nayaka "Keladi Nayaka") and the [Mysore kingdom](/wiki/Mysore_kingdom "Mysore kingdom"), the Badami Chalukya coins being a recent discovery.[[78]](#cite_note-coins-78)[[79]](#cite_note-coins1-79)[[80]](#cite_note-coins2-80) The coins of the Kadambas of Goa are unique in that they have alternate inscription of the king's name in Kannada and Devanagari in triplicate,[[81]](#cite_note-triple-81) a few coins of the Kadambas of [Hangal](/wiki/Hangal "Hangal") are also available.[[82]](#cite_note-han-82)

## Literature

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Kannada&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: Literature")]

Main articles: [Kannada literature](/wiki/Kannada_literature "Kannada literature"), [List of important milestones in Kannada literature](/wiki/List_of_important_milestones_in_Kannada_literature "List of important milestones in Kannada literature"), and [List of notable epics in the Kannada language](/wiki/List_of_notable_epics_in_the_Kannada_language "List of notable epics in the Kannada language")

### Old Kannada

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Kannada&action=edit&section=8 "Edit section: Old Kannada")]

Main articles: [Rashtrakuta literature](/wiki/Rashtrakuta_literature "Rashtrakuta literature"), [Western Ganga literature](/wiki/Western_Ganga_literature "Western Ganga literature"), [Kannada literature in the Western Chalukya Empire](/wiki/Kannada_literature_in_the_Western_Chalukya_Empire "Kannada literature in the Western Chalukya Empire"), [Hoysala literature](/wiki/Hoysala_literature "Hoysala literature"), and [Medieval Kannada literature](/wiki/Medieval_Kannada_literature "Medieval Kannada literature")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/21/11th_century_Someshwara_temple%2C_Lakshmeswar%2C_Karnataka_India_-_89.jpg/250px-11th_century_Someshwara_temple%2C_Lakshmeswar%2C_Karnataka_India_-_89.jpg)](/wiki/File:11th_century_Someshwara_temple,_Lakshmeswar,_Karnataka_India_-_89.jpg)

[Shankha Jain Basadi](/wiki/Lakshmeshwara_Jain_temples "Lakshmeshwara Jain temples") temple at Lakshmeshwar where the notable [Adikavi Pampa](/wiki/Adikavi_Pampa "Adikavi Pampa") wrote the [Adipurana](/wiki/Adipurana "Adipurana") in Kannada language

The oldest known existing record of [Kannada poetry](/wiki/Kannada_poetry "Kannada poetry") in *Tripadi* metre is the [Kappe Arabhatta](/wiki/Kappe_Arabhatta "Kappe Arabhatta") record of the 7th century AD.[[49]](#cite_note-hal-49)[[83]](#cite_note-poetry-83) *[Kavirajamarga](/wiki/Kavirajamarga "Kavirajamarga")* by King Nripatunga [Amoghavarsha](/wiki/Amoghavarsha "Amoghavarsha") I (850 AD) is the earliest existing literary work in Kannada. It is a writing on literary criticism and poetics meant to standardise various written Kannada dialects used in literature in previous centuries. The book makes reference to Kannada works by early writers such as King [Durvinita](/wiki/Durvinita "Durvinita") of the 6th century and [Ravikirti](/wiki/Ravikirti "Ravikirti"), the author of the Aihole record of 636 AD.[[84]](#cite_note-extinct_works6-84)[[85]](#cite_note-85) Since the earliest available Kannada work is one on grammar and a guide of sorts to unify existing variants of [Kannada grammar](/wiki/Kannada_grammar "Kannada grammar") and literary styles, it can be safely assumed that literature in Kannada must have started several centuries earlier.[[84]](#cite_note-extinct_works6-84)[[86]](#cite_note-extinct_works5-86) An early [extant](/wiki/Extant_literature "Extant literature") prose work, the *Vaḍḍārādhane* (ವಡ್ಡಾರಾಧನೆ) by [Shivakotiacharya](/wiki/Shivakotiacharya "Shivakotiacharya") of 900 AD provides an elaborate description of the life of Bhadrabahu of [Shravanabelagola](/wiki/Shravanabelagola "Shravanabelagola").[[87]](#cite_note-kavirajamarga-87)

Some early writers of prose and verse mentioned in the *Kavirajamarga,* numbering 8–10, stating these are but a few of many, but whose works are lost, are Vimala or Vimalachandra (c. 777), Udaya, Nagarjuna, Jayabodhi, [Durvinita](/wiki/Durvinita "Durvinita") (6th century), and poets including Kavisvara, [Srivijaya](/wiki/Srivijaya "Srivijaya"), Pandita Chandra, [Ravikirti](/wiki/Ravikirti "Ravikirti") (c. 634) and Lokapala.[[88]](#cite_note-jstor-88)[[89]](#cite_note-89)[[90]](#cite_note-:5-90)[[91]](#cite_note-:6-91)[[92]](#cite_note-dandin-92) For fragmentary information on these writers, we can refer the work *Karnataka Kavi Charite*. Ancient indigenous Kannada literary compositions of (folk) poetry like the *Chattana* and *Bedande*, which preferred to use the *Desi* metre, are said to have survived at least until the date of the Kavirajamarga in 850 AD and had their roots in the early Kannada folk literature. These Kannada verse-compositions might have been representative of folk songs containing influence of Sanskrit and Prakrit metrical patterns to some extent. "Kavirajamarga" also discusses earlier composition forms peculiar to Kannada, the "gadyakatha", a mixture of prose and poetry, the "*chattana*" and the "*bedande*", poems of several stanzas that were meant to be sung with the optional use of a musical instrument.[[90]](#cite_note-:5-90)[[93]](#cite_note-93)[[94]](#cite_note-94) [Amoghavarsha Nripatunga](/wiki/Amoghavarsha_Nripathunga "Amoghavarsha Nripathunga") compares the *puratana-kavigal* (old Kannada poets) who wrote the great *Chattana* poems in Kannada to the likes of the great Sanskrit poets like Gunasuri, Narayana, Bharavi, Kalidasa, Magha, etc. This Old Kannada work, *Kavirajamarga*, itself in turn refers to a *Palagannada* (Old Kannada) of much ancient times, which is nothing but the Pre-Old Kannada and also warns aspiring Kannada writers to avoid its archaisms, as per R. S. Hukkerikar. Regarding earlier poems in Kannada, the author of "*Kavirajamarga*" states that old Kannada is appropriate in ancient poems but insipid in contemporaneous works as per R. Narasimhacharya.[[88]](#cite_note-jstor-88)[[90]](#cite_note-:5-90)[[95]](#cite_note-95) Gunanandi (900 AD), quoted by the grammarian Bhattakalanka and always addressed as *Bhagawan* (the adorable), was the author of a logic, grammar and *sahitya*. Durvinita (529–579 AD), the Ganga king, was the pupil of the author of Sabdavatara, i.e., Devanandi Pujyapada. Durvinita is said to have written a commentary on the difficult 15th *sarga* of Bharavi's *Kiratarjuniya* in Kannada. Early Kannada writers regularly mention three poets as of especial eminence among their predecessors – Samanta-bhadra, Kavi Parameshthi and Pujyapada. Since later Kannada poets so uniformly name these three as eminent poets, it is probable that they wrote in Kannada also. Samantabhadra is placed in the 2nd century AD by Jain tradition. Old Kannada commentaries on some of his works exist. He was said to have been born in Utkalikagrama and while performing penance in Manuvakahalli, he was attacked by a disease called *Bhasmaka*.[[88]](#cite_note-jstor-88) Pujyapada also called Devanandi, was the preceptor of Ganga king Durvinita and belonged to the late 5th to early 6th century AD. Kaviparameshthi probably lived in the 4th century AD. He may possibly be the same as the *Kaviswara* referred to in the Kavirajamarga, and the *Kaviparameswara* praised by Chavunda Raya (978 AD) and his spiritual teacher, Nemichandra (10th century AD), all the names possibly being only epithets.[[96]](#cite_note-archive-96)

Kannada works from earlier centuries mentioned in the [Kavirajamarga](/wiki/Kavirajamarga "Kavirajamarga") are not yet traced. Some ancient Kannada texts now considered extinct but referenced in later centuries are *Prabhrita* (650 AD) by Syamakundacharya, *Chudamani* (Crest Jewel—650 AD or earlier) by Srivaradhadeva, also known as Tumbuluracharya, which is a work of 96,000 verse-measures and a commentary on logic (*Tatwartha-mahashastra*).[[97]](#cite_note-extint_works-97)[[98]](#cite_note-extinct_works1-98)[[99]](#cite_note-extinct_works2-99) Other sources date *Chudamani* to the 6th century or earlier.[[92]](#cite_note-dandin-92)[[100]](#cite_note-god2-100) An inscription of 1128 AD quotes a couplet by the famous Sanskrit poet Dandin (active 680–720 AD), highly praising Srivaradhadeva, for his Kannada work Chudamani, as having "produced Saraswati (i.e., learning and eloquence) from the tip of his tongue, as Siva produced the Ganges from the tip of his top-knot." Bhattakalanka (1604 CE), the great Kannada grammarian, refers to Srivaradhadeva's Chudamani as the greatest work in Kannada, and as incontestable proof of the scholarly character and value of Kannada literature. This makes Srivaradhadeva's time earlier than the 6th–7th century AD.[[96]](#cite_note-archive-96) Other writers, whose works are not extant now but titles of which are known from independent references such as Indranandi's "Srutavatara", Devachandra's "Rajavalikathe",[[90]](#cite_note-:5-90) Bhattakalanka's "Sabdanusasana" of 1604,[[84]](#cite_note-extinct_works6-84) writings of Jayakirthi[[101]](#cite_note-:7-101) are Syamakundacharya (650), who authored the "Prabhrita", and Srivaradhadeva (also called Tumubuluracharya, 650 or earlier), who wrote the "Chudamani" ("Crest Jewel"), a 96,000-verse commentary on logic.[[84]](#cite_note-extinct_works6-84)[[92]](#cite_note-dandin-92)[[100]](#cite_note-god2-100)[[102]](#cite_note-102) The *Karnateshwara Katha*, a eulogy for King [Pulakesi II](/wiki/Pulakesi_II "Pulakesi II"), is said to have belonged to the 7th century;[[101]](#cite_note-:7-101) the *Gajastaka*, a lost "ashtaka" (eight line verse) composition and a work on elephant management by King [Shivamara II](/wiki/Shivamara_II "Shivamara II"), belonged to the 8th century,[[103]](#cite_note-early_works3-103) this served as the basis for 2 popular folk songs *Ovanige* and *Onakevadu,* which were sung either while pounding corn or to entice wild elephants into a pit ("*Ovam*").[[101]](#cite_note-:7-101)[[104]](#cite_note-google-104)[[105]](#cite_note-105) The *Chandraprabha-purana* by Sri Vijaya, a court poet of emperor [Amoghavarsha I](/wiki/Amoghavarsha_I "Amoghavarsha I"), is ascribed to the early 9th century.[[90]](#cite_note-:5-90) His writing has been mentioned by Vijayanagara poets Mangarasa III and Doddiah (also spelt Doddayya, c. 1550 AD) and praised by Durgasimha (c. 1025 AD).[[106]](#cite_note-extinct_works4-106) During the 9th century period, the Digambara Jain poet Asaga (or Asoka) authored, among other writings, "Karnata Kumarasambhava Kavya" and "Varadamana Charitra". His works have been praised by later poets, although none of his works are available today.[[91]](#cite_note-:6-91) "Gunagankiyam", the earliest known prosody in Kannada, was referenced in a Tamil work dated to the 10th century or earlier ("Yapparungalakkarigai" by Amritasagara). Gunanandi, an expert in logic, Kannada grammar and prose, flourished in the 9th century AD.[[90]](#cite_note-:5-90)[[92]](#cite_note-dandin-92) Around 900 AD, Gunavarma I wrote "Sudraka" and "Harivamsa" (also known as "Neminatha Purana"). In "Sudraka" he compared his patron, Ganga king Ereganga Neetimarga II (c. 907–921 AD), to a noted king called Sudraka.[[90]](#cite_note-:5-90)[[103]](#cite_note-early_works3-103) Jinachandra, who is referred to by Sri Ponna (c. 950 AD) as the author of "Pujyapada Charita", had earned the honorific "modern Samantha Bhadra".[[107]](#cite_note-107) Tamil Buddhist commentators of the 10th century AD (in the commentary on *Neminatham*, a Tamil grammatical work) make references that show that Kannada literature must have flourished as early as the BC 4th century.[[108]](#cite_note-greek_roman-108)

Around the beginning of the 9th century, Old Kannada was spoken from [Kaveri](/wiki/Kaveri "Kaveri") to [Godavari](/wiki/Godavari "Godavari"). The Kannada spoken between the rivers [Varada](/wiki/Varada "Varada") and [Malaprabha](/wiki/Malaprabha "Malaprabha") was the pure well of Kannada undefiled.[[109]](#cite_note-{{ISBN|978-81-206-0559-6}}-109)

The late classical period gave birth to several genres of Kannada literature, with new forms of composition coming into use, including *Ragale* (a form of blank verse) and meters like *Sangatya* and *Shatpadi*. The works of this period are based on [Jain](/wiki/Jainism "Jainism") and [Hindu](/wiki/Hinduism "Hinduism") principles. Two of the early writers of this period are [Harihara](/wiki/Harihara_(poet) "Harihara (poet)") and Raghavanka, trailblazers in their own right. Harihara established the *Ragale* form of composition while [Raghavanka](/wiki/Raghavanka "Raghavanka") popularised the *Shatpadi* (six-lined stanza) meter.[[110]](#cite_note-hari-110) A famous [Jaina](/wiki/Jainism "Jainism") writer of the same period is [Janna](/wiki/Janna "Janna"), who expressed Jain religious teachings through his works.[[111]](#cite_note-jan-111)

The [Vachana Sahitya](/wiki/Vachana "Vachana") tradition of the 12th century is purely native and unique in world literature, and the sum of contributions by all sections of society. Vachanas were pithy poems on that period's social, religious and economic conditions. More importantly, they held a mirror to the seed of social revolution, which caused a radical re-examination of the ideas of caste, creed and religion. Some of the important writers of Vachana literature include [Basavanna](/wiki/Basavanna "Basavanna"), [Allama Prabhu](/wiki/Allama_Prabhu "Allama Prabhu") and [Akka Mahadevi](/wiki/Akka_Mahadevi "Akka Mahadevi").[[112]](#cite_note-akka-112)

Emperor Nripatunga Amoghavarsha I of 850 AD recognised that the Sanskrit style of Kannada literature was *Margi* (formal or written form of language) and *Desi* (folk or spoken form of language) style was popular and made his people aware of the strength and beauty of their native language Kannada. In 1112 AD, Jain poet Nayasena of Mulugunda, Dharwad district, in his Champu work *Dharmamrita* (ಧರ್ಮಾಮೃತ), a book on morals, warns writers from mixing Kannada with Sanskrit by comparing it with mixing of clarified butter and oil. He has written it using very limited Sanskrit words that fit with idiomatic Kannada. In 1235 AD, Jain poet Andayya, wrote *Kabbigara Kava*- ಕಬ್ಬಿಗರ ಕಾವ (Poet's Defender), also called *Sobagina Suggi* (Harvest of Beauty) or *Madana-Vijaya and* *Kavana-Gella* (Cupid's Conquest)*,* a *Champu* work in pure Kannada using only indigenous (*desya*) Kannada words and the derived form of Sanskrit words – *tadbhavas*, without the admixture of Sanskrit words. He succeeded in his challenge and proved wrong those who had advocated that it was impossible to write a work in Kannada without using Sanskrit words. Andayya may be considered as a protector of Kannada poets who were ridiculed by Sanskrit advocates. Thus Kannada is the only Dravidian language that is not only capable of using only native Kannada words and grammar in its literature (like Tamil), but also use Sanskrit grammar and vocabulary (like Telugu, Malayalam, Tulu, etc.) The Champu style of literature of mixing poetry with prose owes its origins to the Kannada language and was later incorporated by poets into Sanskrit and other Indian languages.[[104]](#cite_note-google-104)[[113]](#cite_note-113)[[114]](#cite_note-114)[[115]](#cite_note-115)[[116]](#cite_note-kannada_literature-116)[[117]](#cite_note-117)

### Middle Kannada

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Kannada&action=edit&section=9 "Edit section: Middle Kannada")]

Main articles: [Kannada literature in Vijayanagara empire](/wiki/Kannada_literature_in_Vijayanagara_empire "Kannada literature in Vijayanagara empire") and [Literature of the kingdom of Mysore](/wiki/Literature_of_the_kingdom_of_Mysore "Literature of the kingdom of Mysore")

During the period between the 15th and 18th centuries, [Hinduism](/wiki/Hinduism "Hinduism") had a great influence on Middle Kannada (*Naḍugannaḍa*- ನಡುಗನ್ನಡ) language and literature. [Kumara Vyasa](/wiki/Kumara_Vyasa "Kumara Vyasa"), who wrote the *Karṇāṭa Bhārata Kathāman̄jari* (ಕರ್ಣಾಟ ಭಾರತ ಕಥಾಮಂಜರಿ), was arguably the most influential Kannada writer of this period. His work, entirely composed in the native *Bhamini Shatpadi* (hexa-meter), is a sublime adaptation of the first ten books of the [Mahabharata](/wiki/Mahabharata "Mahabharata").[[118]](#cite_note-kumar-118)
During this period, the Sanskritic influence is present in most abstract, religious, scientific and rhetorical terms.[[119]](#cite_note-influence-119)[[120]](#cite_note-inf-120)[[121]](#cite_note-sang-121) During this period, several [Hindi](/wiki/Hindi "Hindi") and [Marathi](/wiki/Marathi_language "Marathi language") words came into Kannada, chiefly relating to feudalism and militia.[[122]](#cite_note-122)

Hindu saints of the [Vaishnava](/wiki/Vaishnava "Vaishnava") sect such as [Kanakadasa](/wiki/Kanakadasa "Kanakadasa"), [Purandaradasa](/wiki/Purandaradasa "Purandaradasa"), [Naraharitirtha](/wiki/Naraharitirtha "Naraharitirtha"), [Vyasatirtha](/wiki/Vyasatirtha "Vyasatirtha"), [Sripadaraya](/wiki/Sripadaraya "Sripadaraya"), [Vadirajatirtha](/wiki/Vadirajatirtha "Vadirajatirtha"), [Vijaya Dasa](/wiki/Vijaya_Dasa "Vijaya Dasa"), [Gopala Dasa](/wiki/Gopala_Dasa "Gopala Dasa"), [Jagannatha Dasa](/wiki/Jagannatha_Dasa_(Kannada_poet) "Jagannatha Dasa (Kannada poet)"), Prasanna Venkatadasa produced devotional poems in this period.[[123]](#cite_note-bhakti-123) Kanakadasa's *Rāmadhānya Charite* (ರಾಮಧಾನ್ಯ ಚರಿತೆ) is a rare work, concerning with the issue of class struggle.[[124]](#cite_note-ragi-124) This period saw the advent of *[Haridasa](/wiki/Haridasa "Haridasa") Sahitya* (*lit* Dasa literature), which made rich contributions to *[Bhakti](/wiki/Bhakti "Bhakti")* literature and sowed the seeds of Carnatic music. Purandara Dasa is widely considered the *Father of Carnatic music*.[[125]](#cite_note-pura-125)[[126]](#cite_note-tattu-126)[[127]](#cite_note-kana-127)

### Modern Kannada

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Kannada&action=edit&section=10 "Edit section: Modern Kannada")]

Main articles: [Modern Kannada literature](/wiki/Modern_Kannada_literature "Modern Kannada literature") and [Kannada poetry](/wiki/Kannada_poetry "Kannada poetry")

The Kannada works produced from the 19th century make a gradual transition and are classified as *Hosagannaḍa* or Modern Kannada. Most notable among the modernists was the poet [Nandalike Muddana](/wiki/Muddana "Muddana") whose writing may be described as the "Dawn of Modern Kannada", though generally, linguists treat *Indira Bai* or *Saddharma Vijayavu* by Gulvadi Venkata Raya as the first literary works in Modern Kannada. The first modern [movable type](/wiki/Movable_type "Movable type") printing of "Canarese" appears to be the *Canarese Grammar* of [Carey](/wiki/William_Carey_(missionary) "William Carey (missionary)") printed at [Serampore](/wiki/Serampore "Serampore") in 1817, and the "[Bible in Canarese](/wiki/Bible_translations_into_Kannada "Bible translations into Kannada")" of [John Hands](/wiki/John_Hands "John Hands") in 1820.[[a]](#cite_note-128)[[128]](#cite_note-129) The first novel printed was [John Bunyan](/wiki/John_Bunyan "John Bunyan")'s *[Pilgrim's Progress](/wiki/Pilgrim%27s_Progress "Pilgrim's Progress")*, along with other texts including *Canarese Proverbs*, *The History of Little Henry and his Bearer* by [Mary Martha Sherwood](/wiki/Mary_Martha_Sherwood "Mary Martha Sherwood"), [Christian Gottlob Barth](/wiki/Christian_Gottlob_Barth "Christian Gottlob Barth")'s *Bible Stories* and "a Canarese hymn book."[[129]](#cite_note-130)

Modern Kannada in the 20th century has been influenced by many movements, notably *Navodaya*, *Navya*, *Navyottara*, *Dalita* and *Bandaya*. Contemporary Kannada literature has been highly successful in reaching people of all classes in society. Further, Kannada has produced a number of prolific and renowned poets and writers such as [Kuvempu](/wiki/Kuvempu "Kuvempu"), [Bendre](/wiki/Bendre "Bendre"), and [V K Gokak](/wiki/V_K_Gokak "V K Gokak"). Works of Kannada literature have received eight [Jnanpith awards](/wiki/Jnanpith "Jnanpith"),[[130]](#cite_note-131) the highest number awarded to any Indian language.[[131]](#cite_note-132)

### Dictionaries

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Kannada&action=edit&section=11 "Edit section: Dictionaries")]

The oldest known Kannada-Kannada, dictionary, *Ranna Kanda* was composed by the poet [Ranna](/wiki/Ranna_(Kannada_poet) "Ranna (Kannada poet)") in 996 AD. Other pre-modern Kannada dictionaries include *Abhidanavastukosa*, a dictionary of Sanskrit words for Kannada users, composed in 1054 by [Nagavarma](/wiki/Nagavarma_II "Nagavarma II") and 'Abhinavaabhidaana', by Abhinava Mangaraja (1398).[[132]](#cite_note-133) Missionary [Ferdinand Kittel](/wiki/Ferdinand_Kittel "Ferdinand Kittel") published a Kannada–English dictionary of more than 70,000 words.[[133]](#cite_note-kittel-134) and *A Grammar of the Kannada language in English* (1903).

[G. Venkatasubbiah](/wiki/G._Venkatasubbiah "G. Venkatasubbiah") edited the first modern Kannada–Kannada dictionary, the 9,000-page, 8-volume *Kannaḍa nighaṇṭu*, published by [Sahitya Parishat](/wiki/Kannada_Sahitya_Parishat "Kannada Sahitya Parishat"). He also wrote a Kannada–English dictionary and a dictionary of difficult words.[[134]](#cite_note-135)[[135]](#cite_note-136)

## Dialects

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Kannada&action=edit&section=12 "Edit section: Dialects")]

Main article: [Kannada dialects](/wiki/Kannada_dialects "Kannada dialects")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/6e/Kannada_Dialects.png/250px-Kannada_Dialects.png)](/wiki/File:Kannada_Dialects.png)

Map of Kannada dialects in the state of Karnataka.

There is considerable difference between the spoken and written forms of the language. Modern standard Kannada and its written form, primarily based on the Mysore–Bangalore variety,[[136]](#cite_note-137) is more or less consistent throughout Karnataka.[[137]](#cite_note-138) Spoken Kannada tends to vary from region to region, though most dialects remain mutually intelligible. Linguists broadly group Kannada dialects into Southern, Northern, Central, and Coastal varieties,[[138]](#cite_note-139) though the boundaries between them often overlap.[[139]](#cite_note-140). Northern Kannada dialects preserve older phonological features not found in southern dialects.[[140]](#cite_note-141) Coastal dialects show lexical influence from Tulu and Konkani due to prolonged language contact.[[141]](#cite_note-142)

Dialects of Kannada language fall into include:

Coastal

* [Mangalore Kannada](/wiki/Mangalore_Kannada "Mangalore Kannada")
* Halakki Kannada ([Halakki Vokkaligas](/wiki/Halakki_Vokkaliga "Halakki Vokkaliga") of [Uttara Kannada](/wiki/Uttara_Kannada "Uttara Kannada") and [Shimoga](/wiki/Shimoga "Shimoga")).[[142]](#cite_note-143)
* [Havigannada](/wiki/Havigannada_dialect "Havigannada dialect") (used mainly by [Havyaka Brahmins](/wiki/Havyaka_Brahmin "Havyaka Brahmin"))[[143]](#cite_note-144)
* [Kundagannada](/wiki/Kundagannada_dialect "Kundagannada dialect") ([Kundapura](/wiki/Kundapura "Kundapura"), [Brahmavara](/wiki/Brahmavara "Brahmavara"), [Bynduru](/wiki/Byndoor "Byndoor") and [Hebri](/wiki/Hebri "Hebri"))[[144]](#cite_note-145)
* Nador-Kannada (used by [Nadavaru](/wiki/Nadavaru "Nadavaru"))
* Halegannada (spoken by [Komarpant](/wiki/Komarpant "Komarpant") in [Goa](/wiki/Goa "Goa"), [Uttara Kannada](/wiki/Uttara_Kannada "Uttara Kannada") and [Khanapur](/wiki/Khanapur "Khanapur"), Karnataka.[[145]](#cite_note-146)
* Hatkar - a conservative dialect that preserves many Old Kannada terms, spoken by [Hatkars](/wiki/Hatkar "Hatkar") in the [Nasik district](/wiki/Nasik_district "Nasik district"), Maharashtra.[[146]](#cite_note-147)[[147]](#cite_note-148) Kannada speakers formed 0.12% of Nasik district's population as per 1961 census.[[148]](#cite_note-149)

Northern

* Dharwad ([Dharwad](/wiki/Dharwad "Dharwad"), [Belagavi](/wiki/Belagavi "Belagavi"), [Haveri](/wiki/Haveri "Haveri"), and [Gadag](/wiki/Gadag "Gadag")) - preserves some older phonological features not found in southern dialects.[[149]](#cite_note-150)

South-Western

* [Arebhashe](/wiki/Arebhashe_dialect "Arebhashe dialect") (Spoken in the Gowda community, mainly in [Madikeri](/wiki/Madikeri "Madikeri") and [Sullia](/wiki/Sullia "Sullia"))[[150]](#cite_note-151)

Southern

* [Bengaluru Kannada](/wiki/Bangalore_Kannada "Bangalore Kannada")
* [Banakal Kannada](/wiki/Banakal#Language "Banakal")

## Writing system

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Kannada&action=edit&section=13 "Edit section: Writing system")]

Main articles: [Kannada script](/wiki/Kannada_script "Kannada script") and [Kannada braille](/wiki/Kannada_braille "Kannada braille")

The language uses forty-nine [phonemic](/wiki/Phonemic "Phonemic") letters, divided into three groups: *swaragalu* (vowels – thirteen letters); *vyanjanagalu* (consonants – thirty-four letters); and *yogavaahakagalu* (neither vowel nor consonant – two letters: *[anusvara](/wiki/Anusvara "Anusvara")* ಂ and *[visarga](/wiki/Visarga "Visarga")* ಃ). The character set is almost identical to that of other [Indian languages](/wiki/Languages_of_India "Languages of India"). The Kannada script is almost entirely phonetic, but for the sound of a "half n" (which becomes a half m). The number of written symbols, however, is far more than the forty-nine characters in the alphabet, because different characters can be combined to form *compound* characters *(ottakshara)*. Each written symbol in the Kannada script corresponds with one [syllable](/wiki/Syllable "Syllable"), as opposed to one [phoneme](/wiki/Phoneme "Phoneme") in languages like English—the Kannada script is syllabic.

* [ŋ, ɲ] are allophones before other palatal and velar consonants respectively.
* [Aspirated consonants](/wiki/Aspirated_consonant "Aspirated consonant") very rarely occur in native vocabulary only in a few numerals like the number 9 and 80, which can be written with a /bʱ/, as in *ombhattu, embhattu*. However, it is usually written with a /b/, as in *ombattu, embattu*; they formed from the Proto Dravidian laryngeal \*H, like in Telugu. Most of the aspirated words are loanwords and the aspiration of consonants depends entirely on the speaker and many do not do it in non-formal situations.
* The alveolar trill /r/ may be pronounced as an alveolar tap [ɾ].
* The voiceless retroflex sibilant /ʂ/ is commonly pronounced as a /ʃ/ except in consonant clusters with retroflex consonants.
* There are also the consonants /f, z/ which occur in recent English and Perso-Arabic loans but they may be replaced by the consonants /pʰ, dʒ/ respectively by speakers.[[151]](#cite_note-bhk-152)
* Some northern Kannada dialects have developed a /ts, dz, tʃ, dʒ/ distinction like in Marathi, Telugu and Southern Odia.[[151]](#cite_note-bhk-152)

Additionally, Kannada included the following phonemes, which dropped out of common usage in the 12th and 18th century respectively:

* /[r](/wiki/Voiced_alveolar_trill "Voiced alveolar trill")/ ಱ (ṟ), the alveolar trill.
* /[ɻ](/wiki/Voiced_retroflex_approximant "Voiced retroflex approximant")/ ೞ (ḻ), the retroflex central approximant.

Old Kannada had an archaic phoneme /ɻ/ under retroflexes in early inscriptions that merged with /ɭ/ intervocalically or /r/ in clusters and it maintained the contrast between /r/ (< PD ∗ṯ) and /ɾ/ from (< PD ∗r). Both merged in Medieval Kannada.[[151]](#cite_note-bhk-152)

In old Kannada at around 10th-14th century, most of the initial /p/ [debuccalised](/wiki/Debuccalise "Debuccalise") into a /h/ e.g. OKn. paḍagu, balupu, keḍapu, Kn. haḍahu, baluhu, {keḍavu, keḍahu} "ship, strength, to pull down".[[152]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEKrishnamurti2003120-153)

Historically, the Tamil-Malayalam languages and, independently, Telugu, phonemically palatalised /k/ before a front vowel; Kannada never developed such phonemic palatalisation (cf. Kn. /kiʋi/, Ta. /seʋi/, Te. /tʃeʋi/ "ear");[[153]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEKrishnamurti2003128-154) however, *phonetically*, Kannada speakers frequently palatalise velar consonants before front vowels, for example, realising /kiʋi/ "ear" as [ciʋi] and /ɡiɭi/ "parrot" as [ɟiɭi].

### Vowels

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Kannada&action=edit&section=16 "Edit section: Vowels")]

|  | [Front](/wiki/Front_vowel "Front vowel") | | [Central](/wiki/Central_vowel "Central vowel") | | [Back](/wiki/Back_vowel "Back vowel") | |
| --- | --- | --- | --- | --- | --- | --- |
| short | long | short | long | short | long |
| [Close](/wiki/Close_vowel "Close vowel") | [i](/wiki/Close_front_unrounded_vowel "Close front unrounded vowel") (ಇ) | [iː](/wiki/Close_front_unrounded_vowel "Close front unrounded vowel") (ಈ) |  |  | [u](/wiki/Close_back_rounded_vowel "Close back rounded vowel") (ಉ) | [uː](/wiki/Close_back_rounded_vowel "Close back rounded vowel") (ಊ) |
| [Mid](/wiki/Mid_vowel "Mid vowel") | [e](/wiki/Close-mid_front_unrounded_vowel "Close-mid front unrounded vowel") (ಎ) | [eː](/wiki/Close-mid_front_unrounded_vowel "Close-mid front unrounded vowel") (ಏ) |  |  | [o](/wiki/Close-mid_back_rounded_vowel "Close-mid back rounded vowel") (ಒ) | [oː](/wiki/Close-mid_back_rounded_vowel "Close-mid back rounded vowel") (ಓ) |
| [Open](/wiki/Open_vowel "Open vowel") |  | | [a](/wiki/Open_front_unrounded_vowel "Open front unrounded vowel") (ಅ) | [aː](/wiki/Open_front_unrounded_vowel "Open front unrounded vowel") (ಆ) |  |  |

* /ɐ/ and /aː/ are phonetically central [[ɐ](/wiki/Near-open_central_vowel "Near-open central vowel"), [äː](/wiki/Open_central_unrounded_vowel "Open central unrounded vowel")]. /ɐ/ may be as open as /aː/ ([[ä](/wiki/Open_central_unrounded_vowel "Open central unrounded vowel")]) or higher [[ɐ](/wiki/Near-open_central_vowel "Near-open central vowel")].
* The vowels /i iː e eː/ may be preceded by /j/ and the vowels /u uː o oː/ may be preceded by /ʋ/ when they are in an initial position.
* The short vowels /a i u e o/, when in an initial or a medial position tend to be pronounced as [ɐ ɪ ʊ ɛ ɔ]. In a final position, this phenomenon occurs less frequently.
* /æː/ occurs in English loans but can be switched with /aː/ or /ja:/.[[151]](#cite_note-bhk-152)

At around the 8th century, Kannada raised the vowels e, o to i, u when before a short consonant and a high vowel, before written literature emerged in the language, e.g. Kn. kivi, Ta. cevi, Te. cevi "ear".[[154]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEKrishnamurti2003106-155)

### Colloquial speech

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Kannada&action=edit&section=17 "Edit section: Colloquial speech")]

Sources:[[155]](#cite_note-156)[[156]](#cite_note-157)[[157]](#cite_note-158)

* Initial i/e have a y- onset and w- for u/o.
* In many dialects, e/o gets lowered to [æ, ɔ] when followed by non high vowels. Some dialects have /a/ as [ə] when a high vowel comes after it and [a] elsewhere.
* Final -e's become i's, in the south its mostly with verbs but in the north it happens everywhere, eg. bere > bære > bæri. This along with the previous change can create some surface minimal pairs, eg. [bæ:ɖə] "don't" vs [be:ɖə] "ask!" (conj of /be:ɖu/).

## Grammar

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Kannada&action=edit&section=18 "Edit section: Grammar")]

Main article: [Kannada grammar](/wiki/Kannada_grammar "Kannada grammar")

The canonical word order of Kannada is [SOV](/wiki/Subject%E2%80%93object%E2%80%93verb "Subject–object–verb") (subject–object–verb), typical of Indian languages.
Kannada is a highly [inflected](/wiki/Inflection "Inflection") language with three [genders](/wiki/Grammatical_gender "Grammatical gender") (masculine, feminine, and neuter or common) and two numbers (singular and plural). It is inflected for gender, number and tense, among other things. The first available Kannada book, a treatise on poetics, rhetoric and basic grammar is the *[Kavirajamarga](/wiki/Kavirajamarga "Kavirajamarga")* from 850 AD.

The most influential account of Kannada grammar is [Keshiraja](/wiki/Keshiraja "Keshiraja")'s *[Shabdamanidarpana](/wiki/Shabdamanidarpana "Shabdamanidarpana")* (c. 1260 AD).[[158]](#cite_note-GS_Gai-159)[[159]](#cite_note-F._Kittel,_'A_Grammar_of_the_Kannada_Language'-160) The earlier grammatical works include portions of *[Kavirajamarga](/wiki/Kavirajamarga "Kavirajamarga")* (a treatise on *alańkāra*) of the 9th century, and *Kavyavalokana* and *Karnatakabhashabhushana* (both authored by [Nagavarma II](/wiki/Nagavarma_II "Nagavarma II") in the first half of the 12th century).[[159]](#cite_note-F._Kittel,_'A_Grammar_of_the_Kannada_Language'-160)

### Compound bases

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Kannada&action=edit&section=19 "Edit section: Compound bases")]

Compound bases, called *samāsa* in Kannada, are a set of two or more words compounded together.[[160]](#cite_note-161) There are several types of compound bases, based on the rules followed for compounding. The types of compound bases or samāsas: tatpurusha, karmadhāraya, dvigu, bahuvreehi, anshi, dvandva, kriya and gamaka samāsa.[*[clarification needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Please_clarify "Wikipedia:Please clarify")*] Examples: *taṅgāḷi*, *hemmara*, *kannusanne*.

### Pronouns

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Kannada&action=edit&section=20 "Edit section: Pronouns")]

In many ways the third-person pronouns are more like demonstratives than like the other pronouns. They are pluralised like nouns and the first- and second-person pronouns have different ways to distinguish number.[[161]](#cite_note-162)

## Significance to modern linguistics

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Kannada&action=edit&section=21 "Edit section: Significance to modern linguistics")]

While an early account of Kannada's grammar is available in *[Shabdamanidarpana](/wiki/Shabdamanidarpana "Shabdamanidarpana"),* it has played a central role in the modern linguistics thanks to its unique semantic and syntactic properties that have been significant to studies of language [acquisition](/wiki/Language_acquisition "Language acquisition") and [innateness](/wiki/Innateness_hypothesis "Innateness hypothesis"). [Jeff Lidz](/wiki/Jeffrey_Lidz "Jeffrey Lidz") is a significant Western linguist to have studied Kannada. His investigations found at least two properties of Kannada to be very impactful in developing contemporary understandings of language acquisition. The first observation was that Kannada has a [causative](/wiki/Causative "Causative") [morpheme](/wiki/Morpheme "Morpheme") (like -*ify* for English, in *personify* or *deify*), which appears whenever a verb with causative meaning is expressed.[[162]](#cite_note-:1-163) This was significant, because it allowed him to test whether an observation of English-learning infants, that they worked out novel verb meanings based on the number of overt [NPs](/wiki/Noun_phrase "Noun phrase") they took, applied cross-linguistically. Given that the presence of the aforementioned causative morpheme would be a more obvious and reliable indicator for differentiating meanings, Kannada was a perfect language to test this observation; Lidz et al. (2003) found that Kannada-learning infants relied *more heavily* on the number of overt NPs than the presence of the causative morpheme.[[163]](#cite_note-164) This has been used by [generativists](/wiki/Generative_grammar "Generative grammar") and [UG](/wiki/Universal_grammar "Universal grammar") [nativists](/wiki/Psychological_nativism "Psychological nativism") to argue that verb meaning acquisition based on [syntactic bootstrapping](/wiki/Syntactic_bootstrapping "Syntactic bootstrapping") is [language universal](/wiki/Linguistic_universal "Linguistic universal") and innate.[[162]](#cite_note-:1-163)

The second property of major significance to develop in modern linguistic understandings lies in the fact that in Kannada, negation comes at the end of the sentence and the quantified object linearly precedes it. This means there is no capacity for confounding linear order and hierarchical relations, as there is in English. This can be used to test whether the observation for English-speaking infants of considering [hierarchical organisation](/wiki/Hierarchical_organisation "Hierarchical organisation") more than [linear order](/wiki/Linear_order_(linguistics) "Linear order (linguistics)") when deciding [scope ambiguity](/wiki/Scope_(formal_semantics) "Scope (formal semantics)") is cross-linguistic, or just a product of English's confounded linear order. Specifically, analysing the sentence "I didn't read two books" (in Kannada), if what matters is linear order, Kannada speaking children's preferred interpretation would be one where 'two books' has wider scope than negation (i.e., there are two books I did not read), and if what matters is hierarchical organisation, their preferred interpretation would be the opposite (i.e., that it is not the case that I read two books). [Lidz](/wiki/Jeffrey_Lidz "Jeffrey Lidz") and Musolino (2002) found that they prefer the second, hierarchical interpretation, just like English-speaking children.[[164]](#cite_note-165) This has been used to argue that infants universally represent sentences not as mere strings of adjacent words, but as hierarchical objects, a regular talking point among [Chomskyans](/wiki/Generative_grammar "Generative grammar") and nativists.[[162]](#cite_note-:1-163)
